Senior Product Owner Home Assignment

bolttech Insurance Results Page Redesign

A product case study focused on improving decision confidence in an embedded insurance Results Page through coverage transparency, optional plan comparison, and a scoped MVP.

📱 Mobile Prototype💻 Web Prototype🎨 Open Miro Board

Contents

Business ObjectiveUser ObjectiveProblem StatementSupporting DataPersonasPossible SolutionsCompetitor BenchmarkingProposed SolutionUser StoriesWireframesPrototype NotePrioritizationStakeholder EmailTeam Health

1. Business Objective

Improve the Results Page so customers can choose an insurance plan with more confidence and less guesswork. This is both a conversion problem and a quality-of-selection problem: more users should select a plan, and fewer should cancel later because coverage was misunderstood.

Product call: keep price visible, but stop making price the only thing that is easy to understand.
  • Increase Results Page to Plan Selection conversion.
  • Reduce drop-off during plan evaluation.
  • Reduce support contacts caused by coverage confusion.
  • Reduce early cancellations caused by misunderstanding coverage.
  • Create a repeatable plan comparison structure across carriers.

2. User Objective

Help customers answer three questions before selecting a plan: What is covered? What is not covered? How is this plan different from another plan?

3. Problem Statement

After the quote flow, the customer reaches a Results Page showing carrier name, plan label, and price. That is not enough to answer the real buying question: what am I actually getting for this price?

Carrier labels are not always apples-to-apples. A Standard plan from one insurer may have different limits, exclusions, and benefits than a Standard plan from another insurer.

Core problem: This is not mainly a choice problem. It is a confidence problem.

4. Data Points Supporting the Problem Statement

“I saw three plans, but I had no idea what the difference was. I just picked the cheapest one and hoped for the right choice.”
“I didn’t know if my new car was even covered for the full value. There was no explanation - just a number and a button.”
“I wanted to compare what was included, but there was no way to do that on the page. I had to Google it separately.”
SignalInterpretation
Users compare primarily on pricePrice is easiest to understand, not necessarily the best decision input.
Users call support after selectingThe UI is not carrying enough explanation for self-serve selection.
30-day cancellationsCustomers may be buying plans that do not match expectations.

5. User Personas

👤

Priya

First-time insurance buyer. Needs simple explanations and confidence that the vehicle is protected.

MVP fit: Coverage drawer, info icons, plain-English summaries.

Watchout: Drawer content must not become too technical.

👤

Marcus

Price-sensitive buyer. Wants speed and affordable monthly premium.

MVP fit: Direct Select Plan CTA while still showing key coverage trade-offs.

Watchout: Low price should not hide low coverage.

👤

Anika

Research-oriented buyer. Wants to compare before committing.

MVP fit: Two-plan side-by-side comparison with separate Select CTAs.

Watchout: Future iterations may need filters and more than two-plan comparison.

6. Possible Solutions Considered

SolutionProsCons / RiskDecision
Information / Coverage DrawerDirectly answers what am I buying?Needs normalized and approved content.Include
Side-by-Side ComparisonHelps users compare beyond price.Can overwhelm on mobile if too much data is shown.Include
Recommendation Badge / Best ValueCould reduce decision fatigue.Needs ranking logic and compliance review.Defer
Carrier Reviews / Trust SignalsMay increase trust.8 SP, legal/content risk, not the primary blocker.Push back
Filters and SortingUseful once plan volume grows.Adds complexity without solving the core confusion.Phase 2
Guided Questionnaire / Recommended Carrier FirstCould reduce cognitive load.Changes journey and needs recommendation logic.Future discovery

7. Competitor Benchmarking & Market Validation

The benchmarking objective was to validate whether coverage transparency and plan comparison are established market patterns in digital insurance shopping.

Competitor / PatternObserved PatternMVP Validation
PolicybazaarMulti-insurer marketplace, policy type education, coverage benefits and comparison messaging.Supports coverage transparency and comparison.
MoneySuperMarketQuote form, compare quotes, choose policy, add-on explanations.Supports plain-language benefit explanation before selection.
Compare the MarketComparison service, cover-type education, customer rating/trust cues.Supports combining education, comparison, and trust-building.
ACKO / digital-first insurersSimplified insurance explanations and direct digital purchase.Supports simple copy and self-serve plan selection.
Insurify / Compare.com patternOnline quote comparison with profile-based carrier options.Supports carrier comparison and quote personalization.
Conclusion: Coverage transparency and plan comparison are not speculative MVP features. Reviews, filters, sorting, and recommendations remain valuable future enhancements but are not required to validate the core hypothesis.

8. Proposed Solution

Information / Coverage Drawer

  • Plain-language summary
  • What is covered
  • What is not covered
  • Coverage limit
  • Deductible
  • Policy link
  • Info icons

Plan Comparison

  • Compare up to two plans
  • Monthly price
  • Coverage tier
  • Coverage limit
  • Collision / Theft / Roadside / Rental car
  • Separate Select buttons
Results
Expand Carrier
View Coverage
Compare Plans
Select Plan

9. Detailed User Stories

US-001: View Coverage Details

As a customer comparing insurance plans, I want to view detailed coverage information, so that I can understand what is included and excluded before buying.

Key acceptance criteria: every expanded plan has View Coverage, drawer opens without refresh, shows covered/not covered, limit, deductible, policy link, info icons, fallback state, and Select Plan CTA.

US-002: Compare Two Plans

As a customer evaluating options, I want to compare two plans side-by-side, so that I can understand differences before selecting.

Key acceptance criteria: compare checkbox on every plan, maximum two plans, Compare CTA appears after two selections, comparison is plan-to-plan, each column has its own Select button, and missing data does not break layout.

10. Wireframes and Prototype Alignment

The wireframes and HTML prototype follow the same screen map: collapsed carriers, expanded carrier, coverage drawer/modal, comparison screen, and confirmation screen. The visuals below show how the wireframes evolved from the initial mobile and web concepts into the final prototype flow.

Open Miro Board

Wireframe Evolution Summary

StageWhat changedWhy it changed
Initial Mobile Wireframe Started with collapsed carriers, expanded plan rows, and a coverage drawer. Validated the core interaction model: reduce initial overload, then reveal plan-level details progressively.
Responsive Web Wireframe Translated the same flow into a desktop layout with wider plan rows and a modal-style coverage view. Ensured the design works beyond mobile and supports a broader quote-and-buy journey.
Final Prototype Direction Removed recommendation labels, filters, and sorting; added direct Select Plan, info icons, optional comparison, and separate Select buttons in comparison. Kept the MVP focused on the validated problems: coverage clarity and plan comparison.

Stage 1 — Initial Mobile Wireframes

This early exploration focused on the mobile-first flow: all carriers collapsed, one carrier expanded, and a coverage drawer for plan-level detail.

Stage 2 — Responsive Web Wireframes

The same concept was translated to web: carriers remain collapsible, plans are shown in a wider layout, and coverage detail is presented as a modal-style panel.

Stage 3 — Final Prototype-Aligned Flow

The final prototype refined the wireframes into an end-to-end interaction flow: collapsed carrier list, expanded plan cards, coverage drawer, optional comparison, and plan selected confirmation.

Key Design Decisions from Wireframe to Prototype

  • Kept collapsed carriers: reduces initial cognitive load and supports carriers with different numbers of plans.
  • Kept plan-level expansion: customers buy a specific plan, not only a carrier brand.
  • Changed recommendation labels to future scope: labels such as Recommended, Popular, and Best Cover need ranking logic and compliance review.
  • Added direct Select Plan: supports users who are ready to buy without forcing them into comparison.
  • Added info icons and plain-language explanations: helps users understand insurance terms without leaving the page.
  • Added separate Select buttons in comparison: removes ambiguity when two plans are compared side-by-side.
  • Removed filters and sorting from MVP: these are useful later, but not required to solve the validated problem in this sprint.

Design note: The final design intentionally keeps the page focused on coverage transparency and optional comparison. Additional decision-support features such as reviews, ratings, recommendation badges, filters, and sorting remain future coon usage, compare usage, direct Select conversion, time to plan selection, support contact rate, and 30-day cancellation rate.

12. Prioritization & Product Thinking

ItemSPDecisionReason
Plan comparison8IncludeCore hypothesis and user research fit.
Coverage drawer5IncludeDirectly answers what is included.
Page load fix3IncludeCritical mobile performance issue.
State-specific disclosures2IncludeLegal/compliance requirement.
Total182 SP buffer kept for QA/UAT defects.

Push back hardest: Carrier reviews, because it is 8 SP, legally sensitive, and not directly tied to the validated problem of coverage confusion.

13. Stakeholder Communication Email

Subject: Results Page comparison demo update

Hi Sarah,

Quick update before the business review. The backend integration for plan data is complete, and the static prototype is in good shape for a walkthrough.

The open issue is on the frontend comparison component. It is currently rendering incorrectly on iOS Safari, which is material because Safari represents 61% of our users. The engineering estimate is 3-4 days to fix and regression test it.

My recommendation is that we do not demo the live component in its current state. For the review, I suggest we show the polished static prototype, explain the completed backend data flow, and be transparent about the Safari fix as the remaining blocker before the clickable demo is shared.

Best,
Aman

14. Team Health & Engineering Partnership

Root cause: The team has been operating in delivery-pressure mode without enough product context, outcome visibility, or engineering involvement in shaping the solution.

ChangeWhen / HowWhy
Sprint planning starts with customer contextFirst 10 minutes before tickets.Connects stories to customer problem and target metric.
Mid-sprint product-engineering walkthrough30 minutes around day 4 or 5.Creates space for trade-offs and engineering input.
Personal bar: A well-run sprint is one where every engineer can explain not only what they are building, but which customer problem it solves, what trade-off was made, and how we will know it worked.
